title: Chained comparisons: desugaring
analysis:
The Reify grammar already parses chained comparisons as left-nested BinOp nodes due to left-associative precedence: `a < b < c` becomes `BinOp("<", BinOp("<", a, b), c)`. Currently the compiler compiles this as-is, which would attempt to compare a Bool result (from `a < b`) with `c` — producing a type-nonsensical expression. The fix is to detect this pattern in `compile_expr_guarded()` and desugar it into an And-chain of pairwise comparisons. No grammar, parser, or evaluator changes are needed — the grammar already produces the detectable left-nested structure, and the evaluator already handles `BinOp::And` with short-circuit Kleene logic.

The algorithm works by flattening the left-nested comparison chain from the parsed AST into a flat list of operands and operators, compiling each operand exactly once, then building the And-chain from compiled pairwise comparisons. Middle operands are cloned at the `CompiledExpr` level (safe because Reify expressions are pure/side-effect-free and the evaluator uses content-hash-based caching for dedup).

Key code locations:
- `compile_expr_guarded()` at lib.rs:769 — expression compilation dispatch
- `ExprKind::BinOp` handling at lib.rs:838 — where chain detection will be inserted
- `resolve_binop()` at lib.rs:629 — operator string-to-enum mapping
- `infer_binop_type()` at lib.rs:661 — type inference (comparisons produce Bool, already handled)
- `CompiledExpr::binop()` in reify-types/expr.rs:177 — constructor with content hashing

steps.description: Write failing test: simple two-element chain `a < b < c` desugars to `And(Lt(a,b), Lt(b,c))`. Create new test file `chained_comparison_tests.rs` with `compile_first_template()` helper. Parse+compile a structure with `constraint a < b < c` and assert the constraint expression is `BinOp(And, BinOp(Lt, a, b), BinOp(Lt, b, c))` by pattern-matching on CompiledExprKind.

Implement chain detection and desugaring in `compile_expr_guarded()`. Add helper `is_comparison_op(op: &str) -> bool` that returns true for <, <=, >, >=, ==, !=. Add `flatten_comparison_chain(expr: &Expr) -> Option<(Vec<&Expr>, Vec<&str>)>` that walks leftward through nested BinOps with comparison operators, collecting operands and operators into flat vectors. In the `ExprKind::BinOp` match arm, before existing logic, call `flatten_comparison_chain` — if it returns Some with >2 operands, compile each operand once, clone middle CompiledExprs, and build the left-folded And-chain of pairwise comparisons. Otherwise fall through to existing BinOp logic.
Write failing test: mixed comparison operators `a < b <= c` desugars to `And(Lt(a,b), Le(b,c))`. Verify both operator variants are preserved correctly in the desugared output.
Verify step-3 test passes with existing implementation (should pass already since flatten handles all comparison ops). If not, fix the operator handling in the flatten/desugar logic.

Write failing test: three-operator chain `a < b < c < d` desugars to `And(And(Lt(a,b), Lt(b,c)), Lt(c,d))`. Verify the recursive left-folded And structure with 3 pairwise comparisons sharing middle operands.
Verify step-5 test passes with existing implementation (should pass since flatten handles arbitrary depth). If not, fix the And-chain construction to left-fold correctly.
Write failing test: equality chain `a == b == c` desugars to `And(Eq(a,b), Eq(b,c))`. Confirms that equality operators participate in chaining per the spec.
Verify step-7 test passes. If not, ensure == and != are included in is_comparison_op().

Write test: middle expression compiled once — verify that a chain like `a < b + c < d` compiles correctly with the complex middle expression `b + c` appearing as a BinOp(Add) in both the right side of the first comparison and the left side of the second. Assert both sub-expressions have the same content_hash, confirming they are cloned from a single compilation rather than compiled independently.
Verify step-9 test passes. The flatten-then-compile-once approach should naturally ensure middle operands are compiled once and cloned. If the content_hash assertion fails, fix the clone logic.

Write test: non-comparison binary op on left does NOT trigger chaining. E.g., `(a + b) < c` should compile as a normal `Lt(Add(a,b), c)` without any And-wrapping. Verifies that only comparison operators in the left operand trigger the chain detection.
Verify step-11 passes. The is_comparison_op() check should naturally exclude arithmetic ops. If not, fix the predicate.
Write test: single comparison (no chain) `a < b` still compiles as plain `Lt(a,b)`. Regression test ensuring the desugaring only activates for actual chains (>2 operands), not single comparisons.
Verify step-13 passes. The flatten function should return None for single comparisons (only 2 operands). If not, adjust the threshold check.

Write integration test: chained comparison in a constraint with Scalar quantities — `constraint 2mm < thickness < 10mm`. Compile the structure and verify: (1) no error diagnostics, (2) the constraint is an And of two comparisons, (3) result_type is Bool. This tests the real-world use case of range constraints in engineering.
Verify step-15 passes. May require no changes if types flow correctly through the desugared form. If dimension checking triggers false errors on the desugared form, investigate and fix.
Write test: chained comparison in a let binding — `let in_range = 0 < x < 100`. Verify the let cell's initializer expression is desugared to an And-chain and has type Bool.
Verify step-17 passes. Let-binding compilation uses the same compile_expr_guarded() path, so desugaring should apply automatically.

Write test: cross-precedence chain `a < b == c` (< at grammar prec 4, == at prec 3). Due to grammar precedence, this parses as `(a < b) == c`, which the compiler should desugar to `And(Lt(a,b), Eq(b,c))`. Verify correct desugaring across precedence boundaries.
Verify step-19 passes. The left-flattening approach handles this since (a < b) is a comparison in the left position of == c. If not, fix.

design_decisions.decision: Compiler-only desugaring — no grammar or parser changes
design_decisions.rationale: The Tree-sitter grammar already parses chained comparisons as left-nested BinOp nodes due to prec.left(4) for ordering ops and prec.left(3) for equality ops. The compiler can detect this pattern (comparison op with comparison on left side) and desugar without any new AST variants or grammar rules. This minimizes the change surface and avoids touching the parser crate.

Only detect chains by walking leftward through nested BinOps. This handles all cases the grammar produces for same-precedence chains (a < b < c) and higher-to-lower precedence chains (a < b == c). The right-nested case (a == (b < c)) is NOT treated as a chain because it arises from different precedence levels and the user likely intends to compare a value with a boolean result. This matches Python's chaining semantics and natural mathematical reading.

design_decisions.decision: Clone middle CompiledExprs instead of adding synthetic let bindings
design_decisions.rationale: Reify expressions are pure (no side effects), so cloning middle operands at the CompiledExpr level is semantically equivalent to single-evaluation. The evaluator's content-hash-based caching ensures that identical sub-expressions (same content_hash) are efficiently deduplicated at evaluation time. Adding a Let variant to CompiledExprKind would require changes to the evaluator (reify-expr) and constraint solver (reify-constraints), which is disproportionate for this feature. The compile-once-then-clone approach naturally avoids re-compilation of middle expressions while keeping the compiled representation flat.

design_decisions.decision: All six comparison operators participate in chaining
design_decisions.rationale: Per the task spec ('All comparison operators may chain'), the operators <, <=, >, >=, ==, != all participate in is_comparison_op(). This enables useful patterns like `a == b == c` (all pairwise equal), `a < b == c` (a less than b, and b equals c), and mixed ordering/equality chains.

For a chain `a op1 b op2 c op3 d`, the result is `And(And(op1(a,b), op2(b,c)), op3(c,d))`. Left-folding matches the natural left-to-right evaluation order and is consistent with how the evaluator's short-circuit And works — earlier comparisons are evaluated first, and if any fails, later ones are skipped via Kleene logic.

reuse.how: Used to convert string operators to BinOp enum when building the desugared comparison pairs. Already handles all six comparison operators plus logical ops.
Used to build both the pairwise comparison nodes (Lt, Le, etc.) and the And-chain nodes. Automatically computes content hashing for deduplication.
Already returns Type::Bool for all comparison and logical operators. Used for each desugared BinOp node to produce correct result types.
Reuse this parse-compile-extract-template pattern in the new test file. Standard pattern across 6+ existing test files.
Reuse the nested match + assert pattern for inspecting compiled expression trees. Standard assertion pattern for verifying compiled expression structure.
The parsed AST stores operators as strings, which is what is_comparison_op() will match against. No new enum needed — just string matching on the existing op field.
